evidence_text: Across their evaluated benchmarks, the automatic evaluators achieved 74.4% to 92.9% agreement with oracle evaluation metrics or human judgments.
source_excerpt: “We validate the performance of these models in several popular benchmarks for digital agents, finding between 74.4 and 92.9% agreement with oracle evaluation metrics.”
note:
The abstract states agreement with oracle metrics; later sections report Android results against human judgments.
```
caveat:
The abstract states agreement with oracle metrics; later sections report Android results against human judgments.
```

evidence_text: On WebArena, the end-to-end GPT-4V evaluator reached 80.6% accuracy against the oracle evaluator.
source_excerpt: “The end-to-end approach with GPT-4V achieves 80.6% accuracy” and Table 1 lists “WebArena (%) 80.6” under GPT-4V.
note:
Accuracy is reported on sampled WebArena trajectories from the GPT-4-based policy.
```
caveat:
Accuracy is reported on sampled WebArena trajectories from the GPT-4-based policy.
```

evidence_text: On WebArena, the modular Captioner + GPT-4 evaluator reached 82.1% accuracy against the oracle evaluator.
source_excerpt: “replacing Mixtral with GPT-4 achieves the highest accuracy at 82.1%” and Table 1 lists “WebArena (%) 82.1” under Captioner + GPT-4.
note:
This is the highest WebArena accuracy among the three evaluator variants reported in Table 1.
```
caveat:
This is the highest WebArena accuracy among the three evaluator variants reported in Table 1.
```

evidence_text: On WebArena, the modular Captioner + Mixtral evaluator matched the oracle evaluator on 74.4% of tasks.
source_excerpt: “Captioner + Mixtral, which uses only open-weight models, matches the oracle’s evaluations for 74.4% of tasks” and Table 1 lists “WebArena (%) 74.4”.
note:
The paper explicitly identifies this variant as using only open-weight models.
```
caveat:
The paper explicitly identifies this variant as using only open-weight models.
```

evidence_text: On Android-in-the-Wild, the Captioner + Mixtral evaluator achieved 92.9% accuracy against human judgments.
source_excerpt: “the Captioner + Mixtral variant shows the highest agreement with human judgment at 92.9% accuracy” and Table 1 lists “Android (%) 92.9”.
note:
This is the highest Android accuracy among the three evaluator variants reported in Table 1.
```
caveat:
This is the highest Android accuracy among the three evaluator variants reported in Table 1.
```

evidence_text: On Android-in-the-Wild, the end-to-end GPT-4V evaluator achieved 90.6% accuracy against human judgments.
source_excerpt: “the end-to-end approach of GPT-4V achieves 90.6% accuracy” and Table 1 lists “Android (%) 90.6”.
note:
The Android evaluation aggregates trajectories from four policies.
```
caveat:
The Android evaluation aggregates trajectories from four policies.
```

evidence_text: On Android-in-the-Wild, the Captioner + GPT-4 evaluator achieved 89.8% accuracy against human judgments.
source_excerpt: “replacing Mixtral with GPT-4 leads to a performance drop to 89.8%” and Table 1 lists “Android (%) 89.8”.
note:
The paper frames this as a drop relative to Captioner + Mixtral on Android.
```
caveat:
The paper frames this as a drop relative to Captioner + Mixtral on Android.
```

evidence_text: Using the evaluator as the reward function in Reflexion improved the best-performing GPT-4 WebArena agent’s success rate by up to 29% relative.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Abstract and Section 4.2, Reflexion on WebArena
source_excerpt: “Without any additional supervision, we improve state-of-the-art performance by 29% on the popular benchmark WebArena” and “the evaluator enhances the best-performing GPT-4 WebArena agent’s success rate by up to 29% of relative improvement.”
note:
The paper attributes this result to inference-time refinement with Reflexion.
```
caveat:
The paper attributes this result to inference-time refinement with Reflexion.
```

evidence_text: In WebArena Reflexion experiments, the Captioner + Mixtral evaluator improved the agent’s relative success rate by 16%.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Section 4.2, Reflexion on WebArena
source_excerpt: “We see the improvement our evaluators provide scales favorable with evaluator capability, with Captioner + Mixtral improves agent’s relative success rate by 16% and GPT-4V based evaluator by 29%.”
note:
This result is reported as lower than the GPT-4V-based evaluator’s improvement.
```
caveat:
This result is reported as lower than the GPT-4V-based evaluator’s improvement.
```

evidence_text: The authors’ preliminary study suggests false negatives hurt Reflexion performance more than false positives.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Section 4.2, Reflexion on WebArena
source_excerpt: “Our preliminary study suggests that false negative evaluations have a more detrimental impact on agent’s performance compared to false positives.”
note:
The paper gives a directional claim from a “preliminary study,” not a full quantitative analysis.
```
caveat:
The paper gives a directional claim from a “preliminary study,” not a full quantitative analysis.
```

evidence_text: The paper explains the asymmetry in Reflexion noise by stating that a false negative forces a retry of a successful execution, while a false positive mainly removes a retry opportunity.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Section 4.2, Reflexion on WebArena
source_excerpt: “If our evaluator predicts an execution is incorrect, but it was actually successful, this forces the agent to retry a successful execution, which nearly always leads a subsequent failure. In contrast, false positives only lose out on the opportunity to retry.”
note:
This is the mechanism the paper gives for why false negatives are more damaging.
```
caveat:
This is the mechanism the paper gives for why false negatives are more damaging.
```

evidence_text: In the iOS domain-transfer setting, filtered behavior cloning with the evaluator improved CogAgent from 8 successful tasks to 14 out of 52, a 75% relative improvement.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Abstract and Section 4.2, Filtered Behavior Cloning on iOS/Table 2
source_excerpt: “we see relative improvements of 75% accuracy in this domain,” “Filtered BC with our evaluator significantly improved the policy model’s performance from 8 to 14 successes, marking a 75% relative improvement,” and Table 2 lists baseline 8 and filtered BC 14.

evidence_text: In the same iOS experiment, self-training improved CogAgent from 8 successful tasks to 11 out of 52.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Section 4.2, Filtered Behavior Cloning on iOS/Table 2
source_excerpt: “Self-training improves over the baseline by 3 tasks” and Table 2 lists “CogAgent Baseline 8” and “+ Self-training 11”.
note:
This is reported as a baseline comparison against filtered BC.
```
caveat:
This is reported as a baseline comparison against filtered BC.
```

evidence_text: About 36% of the human demonstrations annotated for the sampled Android-in-the-Wild tasks were judged unsuccessful.
source_excerpt: “our analysis reveals that about 36% of the human demonstrations we annotate are actually unsuccessful, with common errors including early stopping, completing the wrong task, and making mistakes with respect to the parameters of the task.”
note:
This creates tension with treating human demonstrations as reliable references for evaluation or training.
```
caveat:
This creates tension with treating human demonstrations as reliable references for evaluation or training.
```

evidence_text: On sampled Android-in-the-Wild policies, all three evaluators had 100% Kendall correlation with human judges, while action matching had 66.7%.
source_excerpt: “Quantitatively, all three evaluators achieve a Kendall correlation of 100% with the human judges, while the action matching score only obtains 66.7%.”
note:
This evidence is relevant because it shows tension between whole-trajectory success metrics and the standard single-step action-matching metric.
```
caveat:
This evidence is relevant because it shows tension between whole-trajectory success metrics and the standard single-step action-matching metric.
```

evidence_text: The error analysis reports that reasoning-process errors accounted for about 50% of sampled failures for GPT-4V/GPT-4-based methods and about 70% for Mixtral-Captioner.
citation: Pan et al., 2024, Section 4.3, Error Analysis
source_excerpt: “Errors in the reasoning process, observed in 50% of cases for GPT-4V/GPT-4-based methods and 70% for Mixtral-Captioner.”
note: This attaches a limitation to evaluator quality and indicates substantial remaining reasoning failure even when perception succeeds.
caveat: This attaches a limitation to evaluator quality and indicates substantial remaining reasoning failure even when perception succeeds.
